data.title: Adobe: Outstanding Business At Outstanding Price
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I find Adobe (ADBE) shares attractive from both fundamental and quantitative sides of analysis. It's therefore surprising to find ADBE among the underperformers. I'm starting coverage with a "Strong Buy" rating due to its strong financial position and excellent financial performance, as well as the growth prospects of the business, whose products are industry benchmarks. A significant undervaluation, according to my method, is an additional bonus.

Thesis Explanation

As always, my opinion is based on an actual analysis of the company's financial statements over the past several months. I use an 8-factor model that allows me to place any business on a 100% scale for evaluation. 100% is an outstanding business from all angles: solid performance, a stable balance sheet, and strong growth. Adobe managed to score 93%. In my articles, I examined about 30 companies, and Adobe stormed to second place in the virtual rankings, surpassing Arista Networks (ANET) and Coca-Cola (KO) (90 out of 100 for both), but behind Copart (CPRT) (100 out of 100).

From a quantitative analysis perspective, Adobe has no weaknesses. Its efficient business model with a subscription system, cloud services, and low operating costs ensures a high profitability level. The net profit margin level is 30%. The accrued income is fully supported by actual operating cash flow.

Despite the company's software operations and close ties to AI development, it primarily leverages the benefits of neural networks through partnerships and avoids the CapEx issues common in the IT industry as of today. As a result, the company's net debt is virtually zero, and debt servicing consumes no more than 3% of operating profit (although net interest expenses are also zero).

Finally, the growth metrics that dominate my final quant ranking are also in good shape. I use U.S. GAAP EPS, and the three-year CAGR is 18.3%, according to my calculations. The projected CAGR for the next three years for net income is around 20%, according to Alpha Spread.

That is, the company is currently growing, and the future is also positive. I usually add a stability ratio to this equation to assess growth areas, representing the average YoY growth over the past 12 quarters divided by the standard deviation. I expect an outstanding business to achieve a value of 1, while Adobe's is only 0.47. However, I still included this value in the overall rating, as I understand that the low meaning is based on just one quarter, ending in March 2024.

Then Adobe wrote off $1 billion as a termination fee related to the cancellation of the Figma deal. Without this unique factor, the company's Stability Ratio would have been 2, and the company would have met all eight criteria for outstanding business status. However, actual results are important, so I erred on the side of caution and calculated the final rating with a slight negative for this.

About Products In Short, Without Exact Numbers

For me, all public companies fall into three groups. The first are companies for which I could become not only a shareholder but also a customer. I like the ideas of Buffett and Lynch that investing in companies whose services you regularly use is much more effective and easier than in others. The second group includes companies whose clients I can't directly become. For example, I recently wrote about Southern Copper (SCCO). But I don't directly consume copper. Only indirectly. Well, you get the idea, I think.

Adobe falls into the third group: companies whose services are available to me, but not relevant. Or even not fully understood. However, for the last 15 months, I've had the opportunity to work on a project that lies in the intersection of marketing and IT, so I have a certain opinion about Adobe products.

I wrote above about a high level of profitability, which in my view reflects a significant competitive advantage. I believe it's long-term. And here's a brief explanation why I think so.

Adobe provides the infrastructure for digital content production. For professional freelancers and businesses. Photoshop, Premiere, and Illustrator are benchmark products in their respective fields. If you work for a large company, you must be proficient in using them; otherwise, you're redundant. An hour of a good specialist's work costs more than a subscription, so the company maintains a low churn rate and has the flexibility to gradually increase the price.

My colleagues, as B2B marketing experts, have spoken extremely positively about the Adobe ecosystem. It's convenient, effective, and has low switching costs. But it's not always accessible to small businesses and individual entrepreneurs.

You might say that neural networks can easily replace the value of products. Believe me, I worked on a project for over a year that was designed to facilitate access to such tools. And so far, this hasn't been possible. I'm talking about the quality. Moreover, Adobe is actively integrating AI solutions to improve operational efficiency.

I was also surprised to learn that Adobe plans to acquire Semrush (SEMR). I've used this product for quite some time and with great success, and I can say it's a very powerful promotion tool and fits perfectly into the Adobe ecosystem. We all remember the experience with Figma, of course, but this is somewhat different.

Lagging The Market

If all the numbers are good, the product is in demand, and the future profit track is positive, why are we down 43% over the past 12 months when the market is up 13%?

To be honest, I don't have a definitive answer. But I have noticed that after five of the last seven earnings releases, the market has opened with a downside gap. Although in all these cases the EPS in the report exceeded expectations.

The answer is obvious: they weren't outperforming enough. I suspect the general sentiment toward the SaaS segment is at play here. I'd venture to say that Adobe may not seem AI-focused enough in today's market. The company doesn't have a multi-billion dollar CapEx, and therefore isn't at the forefront of the AI ​​revolution. And the market, each time it sees impressive numbers in reports, is disappointed, not seeing significant growth. This is my assumption, based on the reaction of the last few quarters.

If we dig a little deeper, Adobe's press releases mention AI and its impact on boosting financial performance, but I don't see any serious specifics or segmentation in this context. Sometimes, this seems like a necessary tone of voice in today's world.

To summarize the reasons for the lag, I see them as follows: 1. The market expects accelerated growth, but is seeing stability. 2. AI hasn't yet translated into concrete figures. 3. The reports meet expectations, but don't reveal a new driver.

Greatly Undervalued Right Now

As a result, we have a non-GAAP P/E of 12.3x and approximately 15.7x GAAP, according to my calculations.

While I don't deny that the company's valuation trend appears to be downward over the past three years, I still maintain that the fair value calculated based on the median valuation is significantly higher.

The historical multiples method suggests a more than doubled upside of 41x fair valuation, which I believe is normal over the past 12 quarters. Currently, the stock is trading at a discount to the median of the bottom third of valuations, meaning I can conclude that ADBE is greatly undervalued right now.

This is typically the case for companies experiencing current problems, at the bottom of their cycles, or with serious concerns about the future. As we can see, Adobe is not one of these companies, at least in my humble opinion. Therefore, I'm observing a rare market inefficiency, where an outstanding company trades at an outstanding price.

I would be grateful if you could bring me down to earth in the comments in case I missed something important.

Risk To The Thesis

I won't list the fundamental risks for any business of this scale, such as geopolitics, currency fluctuations, or slowing growth. The primary, most fundamental, and perhaps only risk to the company's business is AI. Adobe isn't an innovator in this area, but rather a consumer or adopter. Some speculate that many tasks currently done manually could be accomplished in seconds in the future using a trained neural network. This could affect everything currently done in Adobe products, from content creation to promotion and customization. But again, having some experience in this field, I can confidently say that we are still very far from the point where machines will be producing advertising for us at the same level as professionals do today. And at a minimum, if this scenario comes to fruition, Adobe has ample time to adapt and integrate into the new reality.

Bottom Line

ADBE's current situation is extremely rare in my experience. Stocks with strong performance, at levels like ADBE's, usually trade at a premium, and the main challenge is finding a bargain. Here, however, we see strong results across the board from a reporting perspective, a solid fundamental, yet at a significant discount. At times like these, it often feels like something huge is missing, like everything is too good to be true. There's no choice but to brush aside doubts and emotions, acknowledge that a company with a net margin of 30% and a growth rate of ~20% is trading at 15x, and "Buy."

data.title: Grab Holdings: Southeast Asia's Super-App Sets Its Sights On The U.S.
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Grab Holdings Limited (GRAB), I highlighted the company’s strong fundamentals, robust growth, and dominant position in the SEA region, with the potential for them to advance on their inflection point and continue delivering stronger profit and cash flow while still growing their revenue at double-digit rates.

With a solid report released recently, their inflection point accelerating, a big step into the North American market through a recent acquisition, as well as strong 2028 guidance - all while the valuation is getting more and more attractive - I believe Grab is worth upgrading to a Strong Buy.

Internal Developments

Grab’s Q4 report continued to show strong growth, with their revenue increasing 19% YoY, while the Adjusted EBITDA grew by 54% YoY and the Adjusted FCF grew by ~10.15% throughout the quarter, while the FY25 report was even stronger, with the Adjusted FCF growing by ~79% YoY as the company’s turnaround into positive cash flow generation advances alongside the growth in revenue.

As for the outlook, Grab sees 20% to 22% growth in revenue and 40% to 44% growth in their Adjusted EBITDA, while their 2028 targets remain very solid, expecting a 20% CAGR from 2025 to 2028, as well as an Adj. EBITDA of $1.5 billion with an 80% Adj. FCF conversion rate, for an Adj. FCF of roughly $1.2 billion is expected in 2028 based on this. When asked about the relatively uncommon 3-year guidance and its predictability, the company’s CFO said the following during their Q4 Earnings Call:

(...) it's all organic growth. That's what we've given out here today. If you look at the strategy, actually hasn't changed in terms of how we're going to continue that momentum. Just to earlier point of the previous question, we're continuing that momentum in the revenue side of the business. And we feel that the timing is right. The last time that we provided a 3-year guidance was back in September 2022. And now the business has changed. The business has continued to scale, we're seeing a momentum, it's an inflection point. And we are seeing all the ingredients that we've been building over the last 2 years now are coming into action, and 2025 was the year demonstration of that. then we want to continue to expand that.

Financially, based on GRAB’s latest report, we continue to see a strong position, with the current assets covering their total liabilities, with very little debt. As mentioned before, the company had previously issued $1.5 billion worth of convertible notes to fund a potential acquisition, and now they revealed the acquisition of Stash, a US-based digital investing platform.

Stash has over $5 billion in assets under management and >1 million paying subscribers, and Grab plans to continue their US development (potentially being a major first step into the market) while also reinforcing their current SEA operations with robo-advisory tech.

Under the terms of this $425 million enterprise value deal, Grab will initially buy a 50.1% stake (expected to close in Q3 2026) and the remaining stake over the next 3 years. The company’s President & COO also highlighted the following regarding their future path:

Looking ahead to the future, we are investing in the next structural shift in On-Demand services. autonomous vehicles and robotics. In partnership with WeRide, we launched our first AV shuttle service for the public in Singapore. Our position as Southeast Asia's leading on-demand marketplace makes us the preferred commercialization partner for global autonomous technology leaders.

Grab also announced a new $500 million buyback program following the completion of their previous $500 million program, spending $274 million in 2025 (and $226 million in 2024), which offsets their $241 million stock-based compensation in 2025.

Macro Developments

Although not directly related, we could actually see Grab benefit from the weakness seen in the US, as the market could start perceiving a higher level of risk and seek to diversify away from it. A potential rotation away from tech/AI/US into emerging markets, value, etc., is where Grab could stand to win as a healthy, high-growth business in a high-potential region.

This can come as the market would adapt to all the geopolitical uncertainty, the falling dollar, Japan’s economic plans (especially now given its debt reaching a new record) that could further pressure the USD and treasuries since it’s the number one foreign holder, crisis fears, and many other factors that can pressure the market’s expectations and trust, as it takes into account much more than just rate cuts.

Meanwhile, tourism data suggests a continued recovery from the pandemic levels in Asia Pacific, although that’s still down 9% from the 2019 level before the pandemic, so there is still plenty of potential not only to recover but also to grow beyond it, as the region continues to have some of the world’s most popular and affordable destinations, which can benefit Grab significantly.

At the same time, some of Grab’s key countries saw double-digit growth in tourism, while we’re still talking about relative weakness in the US and China, meaning that a recovery in these key source markets can be even more impressive for SEA’s tourism, so Grab can win either from a global rotation/diversification or a US recovery - or both.

Not only can Grab gain a lot from that, but they are also able to gain from the region’s long-term potential, especially given their expansion across different segments. Several sources expect SEA’s GDP to grow by about 6% in 2026, while the ASEAN-6 (i.e., Vietnam, the Philippines, Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, and Thailand) are expected to grow at a very strong 4.8% average (PPP-weighted) from 2025 to 2040, which would be massive compared to developed economies.

Risks Reiterated

Although Grab could stand to benefit from a potential recovery or capital migration, they are still going to depend on the economy, and there is plenty of room for it to get worse before getting any better, with geopolitical tensions between the US and China, for example, having consequences on the region’s tourism and growth as well.

Meanwhile, the region’s significant potential also attracts high competition, and we have to remember that Grab is achieving these results while already holding a very significant leadership position in many of the regions they play in, which could eventually be challenged, resulting in weaker growth rates, lower margins, or even market share losses.

Valuation

For Grab's DCF, I’ll use one that gets us to Enterprise Value to take into account their massive net cash position since I believe it’s important, and since most of their debt has a meaningless effect on it, I’ll assume an Adj. FCF of $290 million in 2025 as the base, minus their $241 million in stock-based compensation, starting with $49 million in Adj. FCF in 2025.

Assuming they can grow it (equally) to the $1 billion they expect in 2028 (after SBC that would continue to fall), which seems achievable given their massive growth potential and expansion, implying a margin of about 20.6% if they manage to grow the revenue at a CAGR of 20% during this time (reaching ~$5.82 billion in 2028).

I’ll assume they can then grow this at a CAGR of 12.5% until 2030 and 10% until 2035 (well below the market’s expectations for EPS), to take into account the growth potential of the region and their aggressive expansion, but also account for competitive pressure, with a 4.5% terminal growth rate given the region’s potential, and a WACC of 9%, slightly above my estimated 8.65% (assumptions: ~8.9% cost of equity, ~3.8% cost of debt), to take into account the risks, with 4.43 billion shares outstanding and $4.75 billion in net cash (under a stricter definition than the number provided by Grab, which includes banking customer deposits) as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an enterprise value of $27.52 billion given their strong assumptions, with an equity value of $32.27 billion, for an intrinsic value of $7.29 per share, which is well above the current $4.23 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

I am upgrading Grab to a Strong Buy rating, as the company remains in a strong position to capitalize on the region’s potential, standing to benefit from a recovery in the global economy and the region’s own tailwinds.

There are certainly risks, as this type of growth potential can be attractive for many other players with deep pockets, but the overall risk-reward level is attractive at current levels, in my opinion.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 9% High Yield Dividend Baby Bond From TPG Mortgage Investment Trust
data.description:
When looking at mortgage REITs, we often cover multiple preferred shares and baby bonds from the same company. It’s often useful to compare preferred shares and baby bonds within the same company.

TPG Mortgage Investment Trust’s (MITT) Baby Bonds

There is one major factor to consider when comparing MITT’s two baby bonds to each other. (MITN) has more call risk than (MITP).

Otherwise, they are very similar with 9.5% coupons.

MITN is slightly less attractive because the call risk today creates the potential for shareholders to suffer a small loss from a call. While the loss would be extremely easy for any investor to bear, it is still mathematically present. Further, it is the most significant difference for investors.

When we put these baby bonds side by side, we can see that MITN will mature slightly earlier. Normally, that would be considered a positive factor. However, because these bonds are trading at a slight premium to call value, even after adjusting for dividend accrual, the ability to be called abruptly is a clear negative.

It may be difficult for an investor to swap between the baby bonds because even a fairly normal bid-ask spread would be enough to completely mitigate the advantage that we see currently. At the time that I am preparing these comments, the difference in the prices is $0.03 and both shares had a yield to maturity of 9.33%.

By the time I added the chart above, both shares were trading at $25.25, and yield to maturity was 9.40% and 9.44%.

Which One Would I Pick?

I would consider both of these bonds to be viable choices, but given the call risk, I would clearly prefer MITP.

What about the “Price to Buy” column? This is where things get a little interesting. MITN technically is closer to the target “Buy Under” price. However, since MITN matures sooner, it has a tighter range.

  • If both shares were $24.93, then MITN would have a slight edge.

  • Whereas when both shares are $25.25, the slight edge goes to MITP.

This is simply what happens when you have two similar shares, but one becomes callable sooner and matures sooner. The one with the earlier dates will gain attractiveness faster when the price falls and lose it faster when the price rises.

In my opinion, these bonds are neither the best nor the worst options within the sector.

MITT Common Stock

Diving into the company, beneath the baby bonds, we have a mortgage REIT that had a reasonable amount of common equity several years ago. However, during the pandemic, they managed to lose the vast majority of their common equity. Eventually, the preferred shares recovered, but there was no realistic path for the common shares to recover from the magnitude of the damage.

In my opinion, the damage encountered by the company was significantly amplified by having their repo agreements with a bank that was being very aggressive about selling off assets that were securing loans.

One of the key questions for investors in the baby bonds, and perhaps the most important question for investors most of the time, is will this company be able to pay off their debts? I believe the answer is yes. While the common shareholders endured an absolutely massive pounding (during the pandemic), the debt holders are in a good position. The baby bonds trade slightly above call value as the market agrees with me that the company should not have a problem paying back their bonds. That does not mean zero risk, but they are not particularly high risk either.

Baby Bond Yields

The yield on these baby bonds is not the highest in the sector, but it is higher than some peers. I own a few of the baby bonds from the other mortgage REITs, and this is an area I follow closely. I believe baby bonds can be an excellent tool for many investors. One of the most appealing aspects of baby bonds is that they trade through the exchanges with a normal ticker rather than just going through dealer markets for many bonds. I strongly prefer the transparency of the way baby bonds are traded.

MITT also has some preferred shares outstanding. Investors may want to compare the preferred shares with the baby bonds to see what works better for them. When the yields are comparable, I will usually favor the baby bonds. Of course, for me, the tax difference is not substantial since I like to be able to trade in this sector actively. Consequently, I usually run my baby bonds and preferred shares through tax-advantaged accounts. The tax-advantaged accounts represent a disproportionately large share of my total accounts because I began maxing them out early, and I was aggressively investing in them for many very positive years.

International Investors

For international investors, it may be more attractive to receive interest payments instead of dividends, so the baby bonds might become even more attractive relative to preferred shares. For domestic investors, and by that I mean anyone within the United States, it may be more attractive to have the preferred shares because of a difference in the after-tax yields. The preferred shares coming from a REIT may result in a lower tax rate due to rules about pass-through income and the 20% deduction, depending on the investor's tax situation. I am absolutely not going to dive into every possible tax situation. That would be a dreadful article.

Conclusion

In my opinion, these baby bonds would be dramatically more attractive if the price slipped by even 2%. That would push the yield-to-call much higher, and it would push the yield-to-maturity materially higher. I am primarily valuing based on yield-to-maturity. However, looking at yield-to-call can add value and is important for situations like this one.

Call risk is the reason I favor MITP over MITN, even though the yield to maturity is the same for both bonds.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Easing Continues
data.description:
Federal Reserve easing continues.

Since November 26, 2025, the Federal Reserve has increased its securities portfolio by $76.5 billion, with $16.6 billion of the increase coming in the banking week ending February 11, 2026.

No further moves have been made by the Fed to lower its policy rate of interest...the Federal Funds rate...and the feeling is that the Fed is under less market pressure to lower the rate even further in upcoming weeks.

Since November 26, 2025, the Fed's securities portfolio has followed the following path.

Over this period of time, the Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve Banks...a proxy for the excess reserves in the commercial banking system...have risen by $60.5 billion.

The increase in these "excess reserves" should help to put downward pressure on the Federal Funds rate.

At this time, however, the increase in these "excess reserves" does not seem to be putting much downward pressure on the Fed's policy rate of interest.

So, the market expectation is for the Federal Funds rate to remain constant for the near future.

Moving over to the performance of the M2 money stock, we see that over the past year or so, the M2 money stock has risen by 5.0%.

Thus, if the velocity of circulation of the M2 money stock remains constant, this rise can be associated with the current rate of growth of the U.S. economy...in the 2.0% to 2.5% range...and this can result in an expected rate of inflation during this time period of 2.5% to 3.0%.

For the year 2025, these numbers seem to be relatively close to what actually happened.

The problem is, however, that there is lots and lots of money "hanging" around in the commercial banking system. Remember, for February 11, 2026, the commercial banking system held almost $3.0 trillion in excess reserves.

These "excess reserves" were injected into the banking system during the protection effort of the Fed in the 2020 time period to combat the disruptions to the banking system caused by the COVID-19 pandemic and following recession.

Overall, this "injection" of funds into the banking system is picked up in the following chart.

As can be seen, in early 2020, in order to fight the spread of the COVID-19 pandemic, the Federal Reserve bought lots and lots and lots of securities.

How this "overhang" is going to impact what happens to the money stock in the near future is totally unknown.

Just be aware that there is plenty of "excess" cash in the banking system that can be lent and turned into "money" in a very short period of time. The Fed doesn't have to go through the process of providing these funds moving into the future.

So, this is the "picture" that current Fed-chairman Jerome Powell is going to be leaving to the nominated Fed-chairman Kevin Warsh.

President Trump wants radically lower interest rates.

My feeling is that any efforts by the Fed to do much more than they are doing now, especially since the Fed has moved back to a stance of "quantitative easing," will turn out to be counterproductive.

My belief is that if the Fed does much more to drive more "excess reserves" into the banking system or to drive down its policy rate of interest, the Fed will "release" the resources to send the level of prices upwards once again and set off higher and higher rates of inflation.

This, I believe, is going to be a very sensitive time for the financial markets.

If market participants develop any kind of a belief that the Fed is going to "err" on the looser side of the monetary policy spectrum, inflation will be set off once again.

The work the Federal Reserve has done to reduce inflationary pressures over the past three and one-half years or so will have been "in vain."

Some possible "stories" of such a drastic downturn are already occurring in the major newspapers, like the Wall Street Journal.

Be aware.

data.title: Grupo Cibest: A Good Diversification Play In The Global Banking Sector
data.description:
Grupo Cibest S.A. (CIB) is an interesting growth play in the global banking sector due to its combination of good growth prospects, high profitability, attractive valuation compared to Latam peers, and a high-dividend yield.

Business Overview

Grupo Cibest is a financial holding company based in Colombia, which was set up as part of the recent reorganization of Bancolombia into a simpler business structure. Grupo Cibest was created as a new parent company holding stakes in several entities across the group instead of being owned directly by Bancolombia. As banks are highly regulated, this new holding structure is expected to give more flexibility for the group to pursue M&A activity, perform share buybacks, and potential divestments in the future. Its current market value is about $20 billion, and its shares trade in the U.S. as an ADR on the New York Stock Exchange.

The group is based in Colombia but is also present in some other Latin American countries, including Panama, El Salvador, and Guatemala. In these countries, the bank has entered the markets through acquisitions between 2007 and 2013.

At the end of last September 2025, Grupo Cibest’s total assets amounted to some $90 billion, and its loan book was around $68 billion, showing that the company has a relatively small size in the global banking sector. Its most important unit is Bancolombia, which is Colombia’s leading financial institution and has a long history, with some 150 years in operation offering banking services to individual and commercial customers throughout the country. It has a market share of about 28% regarding loans and 27% for deposits, showing its strong position in the banking market in Colombia. By segment, about 70% of its earnings are generated by Bancolombia, while other units have smaller weights within the group, and no one generates more than 10% of the group’s earnings.

Regarding its growth strategy, it has been focused on organic initiatives over the past few years, as the group has pushed for growth by investing in new technologies and digital offerings. It has created several digital platforms, such as Nequi, Wenia, and Wompi, offering digital banking services, online payments, and digital assets for its customers. The most interesting one, in my opinion, is Nequi, which is a digital financial platform (neobank) that was launched in 2016 by Bancolombia and has nowadays over 26 million customers. Nequi has reported strong growth in recent years, with its loan book more than doubling over the past year, showing that digital offerings with low costs remain a compelling proposition for customers in Colombia, especially among the underbanked population.

According to BBVA research (BBVA), banking penetration in Colombia is relatively high, with around 95% of the adult population having a bank account. This rate has increased, especially in digital banking services, over the past few years, even though the market penetration is still quite low for some banking products. Indeed, regarding loans, the penetration rate is around 35% for individuals and only 26% for corporations, showing that there is still a large market to tap for both traditional and neobanks operating in the Colombian banking sector.

This means that Grupo Cibest is very well positioned to grow its business over the coming years both through its traditional banking operations and Nequi, due to its leading position in the market and digital offerings. Indeed, according to DataCube Research, the Colombian banking industry value is projected to increase from about $49 billion in 2025 to more than $82 billion by 2033, representing a compounded annual growth rate of 6.8% during this period, boding well for Grupo Cibest’s growth prospects as the leading player in the industry.

Regarding its international operations, Grupo Cibest announced a couple of months ago an agreement to sell Banistmo, its banking operation in Panama, for a total amount of $1.4 billion. This transaction is expected to close during the second half of 2026 and is part of the group’s strategy to focus its operations where it has better growth prospects, namely in Colombia and digital offerings. Banistmo had a below-average profitability within the group and a lower growth history, which explains why Grupo Cibest has decided to monetize this stake.

Further transactions may also happen in the future, as the group’s structural reorganization gives it more flexibility to pursue M&A, both from an acquiring and seller point of view. Despite that, I expect Grupo Cibest to use a large part of sales proceeds to return capital to shareholders and invest in organic growth, which have been two key strategic priorities for the group in the recent past and are expected to remain so in the near future.

Financial Overview

Regarding its financial performance, Grupo Cibest has reported a positive operating performance over the past few years, even though its earnings growth has not been impressive for an emerging markets bank. Indeed, its profitability has declined somewhat in recent years as interest rates were cut in Colombia due to moderating inflation. Despite that, its return on equity (ROE) ratio, a key measure of profitability in the banking sector, has remained above 15% over the past three years, which is a very good level of profitability in the sector.

As the vast majority of Grupo Cibest’s earnings are generated in its domestic market, a key factor impacting its earnings and profitability is the interest rate level in the country. As can be seen in the next graph, Colombia’s central bank performed several cuts since mid-2023, from an interest rate level of 13.25% to a rate of 9.25% by mid-2025, being a headwind for its net interest income (NII) and profitability over the past couple of years.

Despite that, Grupo Cibest has been able to report a resilient net interest margin during the first nine months of 2025, increasing by 20 basis points (bps) compared to year-end 2024 to a level of 6.6%, which can be considered quite good taking into account lower interest rates during the period. This was driven mainly by higher net investment margins, while lending yields were stable in recent quarters. However, this trend is likely to change ahead as Colombia’s central bank has reversed its monetary policy recently.

Indeed, the central bank raised its benchmark rate by 100 bps in January to 10.25% due to the strong performance of the economy, increasing inflation expectations, and a deterioration in the current account balance. Inflation expectations were revised upwards for 2026, from a rate of 4.6% to 6.4%, being a key reason for the central bank to perform its first rate hike in almost three years.

For Grupo Cibest, higher rates can now represent a tailwind for NII growth over the coming quarters, especially when considering that further rate hikes from the central bank are expected during the coming months. Given that usually banks asset yields tend to be more sensitive to rates than liabilities, a rate-hiking cycle is positive for revenue and earnings growth in the near term.

This is a very different outlook from what Grupo Cibest was expecting just some months ago, as the bank’s earnings presentation in Q3 2025 showed inflation expectations of 4.1% and a policy rate of 8.25% in 2026, forecasts that have become outdated quite rapidly.

Not surprisingly, analysts have been revising their revenue estimates higher over recent months, but I think current estimates are still somewhat conservative, as the Street seems to be conservative and is still expecting what the bank has to say about its outlook for 2026. Grupo Cibest is scheduled to report its 2025 earnings next February 23, when it’s quite likely that the bank will provide an updated macroeconomic forecast and new financial targets for 2026. Its last guidance update for the 2026 year was provided during December when it announced the sale of Banistmo, but this seems to be already outdated, as NIM and ROE are, in my opinion, likely to be higher in 2026 than what the bank was expecting at the time.

As can be seen in the previous table, the bank has a very good profile, as loan growth is expected to be in the high single digits during 2026, while efficiency and cost of risk are expected to increase a little bit compared to Q3 2025, but business margins are expected to increase, being an important support for higher earnings during this year.

Regarding its capitalization, its banking subsidiaries all had capital ratios comfortably above the regulatory requirements at the end of last September, while at the holding company level, its double leverage ratio was 106%, which means it has a comfortable capital position. Therefore, the group seems to have plenty of capacity to distribute a large part of earnings to shareholders, which has been its strategy in recent quarters both through dividends and share buybacks. As can be seen from the next graph, the group has been increasing its dividend quite significantly in recent years and has also returned more capital to shareholders through share buybacks as part of its push to increase returns to shareholders and seek a higher valuation in the stock market.

This trend is not expected to change much ahead, given that, according to analysts’ estimates, Grupo Cibest’s dividend is expected to be $4.50 per share related to 2025 earnings, up by 33% YoY, and distribute around $4.78 per share by 2027. Considering its $4.50 dividend per share, Grupo Cibest is currently trading at a forward dividend yield of 5.9%, which seems to be quite attractive for income-oriented investors. Moreover, its dividend seems to be well covered by earnings, given that its EPS estimated for 2025 is $7.81 per share, leading to a conservative dividend payout ratio of 57%.

Regarding its valuation, Grupo Cibest has been re-rated over the past couple of years, like it has happened across the global banking sector, but there still seems to be some value left. Its current valuation is around 1.6x forward book value, a premium to its historical valuation of 0.95x book value over the past five years.

However, compared to Latam peers, such as Banco Macro (BMA), Itaú (ITUB), or Credicorp (BAP), it’s still trading at a discount given that its peers trade on average around 2.3x book value. I see this valuation as attractive also when considering its ROE around 17-18%, with potential to be revised upwards in the near term if rates continue their upward trend in Colombia over the next few quarters.

Conclusion

Grupo Cibest has a leading position in Colombia’s banking sector, being well positioned to deliver strong business growth over the next few years supported by industry tailwinds and its organic efforts both in traditional banking and digital offerings. In the short term, I think the bank can deliver higher returns than what the market is currently expecting, as interest rates are now a tailwind for revenues and profitability, something that doesn’t seem to be fully priced in its share price.

Considering this background, I see Grupo Cibest as an interesting growth play in the global banking sector, offering good diversification to investors who want to have exposure to the banking sector outside of the U.S.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BorgWarner Adds Data Center Power To Its Flexible Growth Strategy
data.description:
The significant negative revisions to EV buildout plans have forced many would-be suppliers to rethink their strategies. BorgWarner (BWA) has chosen to embrace a flexible approach where it maintains its capabilities in EV powertrain components while looking for other market opportunities. The company has started picking up hybrid vehicle awards but surprised the Street with a sizable partnership that targets data center power supply with its preexisting portfolio offerings.

Although BorgWarner did reasonably well in the fourth quarter, it was the data center power announcement that spiked the shares, driving a 22% single-day move and over 50% appreciation since my last article.

I’m concerned that data center hype and elevated expectations will bring quite a bit more volatility into the share price, particularly as deliveries won’t start until 2027 and it will take time to scale up the business. On the other hand, BorgWarner is finally getting more of its due, and I see upside to expectations in both the core auto supply and emerging data center businesses.

A Beat, With Particular Strength in Margins

Stronger-than-expected build rates in the fourth quarter, particularly in North America, already biased auto suppliers like BorgWarner to outperform on revenue in the fourth quarter, but management did particularly well on margins.

Revenue rose 4% as reported and 0.8% in organic terms, good for a 1.5% beat versus the Street. While other auto suppliers have done even better, BorgWarner has been held back (and underperformed underlying production) due to headwinds from canceled EV programs, particularly in the battery business.

Gross margin improved 60 bp yoy and 200 bp qoq to 20.5%, beating by 150 bp, with the company seeing some benefits from compensation from OEM partners on those EV programs that are underperforming, pushed out, or canceled altogether.

Adjusted operating income rose 21%, with margin up almost two points to 12%, beating by 14%. Segment-level income was up 15%, with margin up 140 bp to 14%.

Looking at the segments, Turbos and Thermal Tech was down about 5% (missing by 4%), with segment profits down 11% (a 13% miss) and margin down 170 bp to 14.1%. Drivetrain and Morse Systems revenue rose more than 2% (a 2% beat), with profits up 13% (a 7% beat) and margin up 130 bp to 19.1%. PowerDrive Systems revenue rose 15% (beating by 9%), with profits reversing a year-ago loss to $28 million (a 200% beat) and a margin of 4.5%. Battery and Charging revenue declined 12% (a 19% beat), with a $2 million profit (a 144% beat) and a margin of 1.3%.

Comparable information is very limited right now. Garrett Motion (GTX) hasn’t reported yet, and Valeo (VLEEY) (VLEEF) has given a preliminary guide that suggests basically flat core year-over-year performance in Q4. While BorgWarner hasn’t done as well as many of the other suppliers that have reported, names like Autoliv (ALV), Dana (DAN), and Lear (LEA) aren’t great comps given different business mixes.

Conservative Guidance is Not Unusual

Absent the data center announcement, I think BorgWarner might have traded down on the company’s initial guidance for 2026.

Management guided to a 1.5% to 3.5% organic revenue decline, with a revenue midpoint about 3%-4% below the sell-side average (different sources give different average estimates). Underpinning this guidance was an expectation that passenger vehicle volumes would be flat to down 3%, with net declines in North America (down 3%), Europe (down about 1%), and China (down 2.5%).

This looks conservative next to an updated S&P Global Mobility forecast of global volume down 0.2% (revised up from down 0.4%). S&P is expecting better results across geographies, including North America, down 1.6%; China, down 1.2%; and Europe, down 0.5%.

It’s not unusual for BorgWarner to give conservative initial guidance and deliver beat-and-raise quarters as the year develops. I’d also note that there are some company-specific issues working against the company, including a weaker EV volume outlook.

I would note, though, that the margin guidance was stronger than expected, so the impact to adjusted operating income versus the prior Street expectation is minimal, and the $1 billion midpoint of FCF guidance was slightly above my prior expectation.

Repurposing Technology Into Data Centers

Arguably following somewhat in the footsteps of Accelleron (ACLLY), an established player in large-scale, non-automotive turbocharging, BorgWarner is repurposing a broad selection of its core technologies toward data center power generation.

BorgWarner announced a partnership with TurboCell (part of Endeavour) to provide fuel-flexible primary and backup power (and battery storage) for data centers. These turbine generator systems are purpose-built for data center demands, and BorgWarner will be contributing turbocharging, thermal management, power electronics, and software technology, totaling about two-thirds of the content. Management expects production to launch in 2027, with $300 million in incremental revenue in Year 1.

I like this deal, as it is a pretty simple port of existing capabilities, and BorgWarner is not going to need to invest meaningful R&D or capex to support this project. I also think TurboCell has a credible use case in data centers, as generators from Caterpillar (CAT) and Cummins (CMI) are typically less flexible, less efficient, and more expensive to operate, while larger gas turbines are considerably more expensive upfront and can have challenging form factors (not to mention, demand far outstrips supply today).

BorgWarner could be looking at $1,500/kW or more of content, and if this venture grows to serve 2 GW/year, that would be highly significant incremental revenue at current-or-better margins ($3 billion). Relative to today’s capacity in place, that would be about a 3% share, but with data center capex growing at strong double-digit rates, it’s more like a 2% share of potential 2030 capacity and a 1% share or less of expected 2035 capacity.

Whether this venture can get to 2 GW is a big question—data center operators are often conservative and may need years of testing to validate the generators before ordering at scale. Moreover, many players are getting into data center power (including Wartsila’s (WRTBY) sub-utility-scale gas turbines), and gas turbine suppliers are looking to add meaningful capacity. Still, it’s a move with significant upside potential and not much downside risk.

The Outlook

There is still upside in the core business as well. BorgWarner announced multiple new awards for hybrid and EV vehicles, and I do think the Street underestimates BorgWarner’s leverage to OEMs pivoting away from EVs and toward hybrids as “bridging” options before EVs become more viable at scale in North America and the EU.

I also see M&A optionality. BorgWarner has what it needs in terms of core EV/hybrid powertrain components, but I could see the company considering smaller deals in areas like auto control software (to participate in the move toward software-defined vehicles) and possibly even battery technology on a limited basis. Failing that, substantial FCF generation can repay debt and return to shareholders in dividends and buybacks.

I’m expecting BorgWarner to modestly surpass its target for 2026, but I’m still looking for minimal (sub-1%) revenue growth right now. I expect revenue growth to accelerate toward 4% on improving build rates and more hybrid/EV uptake and accelerate further toward 5% on those drivers and some incremental contributions from data center power. At this point I’m modeling $1B in incremental sales from data center power in 2035.

For margins, I’m now looking for 15.5% EBITDA in 2026 (and just under 11% for adjusted operating margin), improving to 17% over the next five years. This should drive free cash flow margins into the 7%s on a consistent basis (versus around 5.5% historically), supporting around 6% annualized FCF growth and annualized total return potential close to 10%.

I also value auto parts companies based on margin-driven EV/revenue, and while BorgWarner has been trading at a discount due to concerns about the EV business, that gap has largely evaporated. At 1.05x forward revenue (in line with the 15.5% EBITDA margin), BorgWarner should trade in the mid-$60s.

The Bottom Line

While BorgWarner trades much closer to my estimate of fair value than it has in some time, I’m not chomping at the bit to sell out. I still believe the mid-term and long-term potential of the hybrid and EV business is stronger than sentiment reflects, and I think there is upside there.

While the data center announcement is likely to add volatility, and I do see a risk of impatience from the Street regarding the revenue ramp, I like the longer-term potential.

I continue to believe this is a stock worth owning, though a quick move into the $70s without incremental positive news would lead me to at least think about locking in some profits.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CPI Report: The Best News Is Not In The Report
data.description:
The Consumer Price Index (CPI) rose a better-than-expected 0.2% in January, which was the smallest monthly increase since July, leaving the annualized rate at 2.4%, which is the lowest rate since last May. We are trending in the right direction thanks to the decline in energy prices and used vehicles.

The core rate, which excludes food and energy, rose an as expected 0.3% last month, lowering the annualized rate to 2.5%, which is its lowest level since March 2021. This is progress. Shelter costs rose 3% over the prior year, which was down from 3.2%. The continued decline in shelter costs should be the most important factor in reestablishing the disinflationary trend.

As I have discussed many times before, the shelter calculation for rent prices accounts for all rents in force rather than what today’s rent cost is relative to what it was a year ago. In many parts of the country rents are outright declining. Regardless, we need older rental contracts that reflect price increases from nearly a year ago to fall off the rolling 12-month period and be replace with current rates. That should drive inflation lower this year.

There is also the high probability that the Supreme Court will rule against President Trump’s tariffs that fall under the Emergency Powers Act in the coming weeks. That would lower input costs for businesses and prices on certain goods for consumers.

This good news has brought 2-year Treasury yields (US2Y) down to their lowest level since 2022, and as a proxy for where the Fed funds rate will be moving forward, suggests that the Fed will cut at least twice before year end. I maintain that we will see three cuts totaling 75 basis points.

The probably for the next rate cut to occur at the Fed’s June meeting has risen to better than 50% after this morning’s CPI report.

The expectation that lower rates will come sooner has lit a fire under risk assets, with stocks, precious metals, and cryptocurrencies all catching a bid to finish what has otherwise been a rough week, as the deleveraging process and rotation from growth to value ensues. The Russell 2000 (RTY) is leading all indexes, as small caps are domestically focused and the most sensitive to lower borrowing costs. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) is struggling to stay in positive territory.

The best news is not in the report but derived from it. Real wages are determined by the CPI. Today’s Real Earnings Report accounts for the increase in average hourly earnings, as well as the number of hours worked, net the cost of living increase. Today’s inflation report shows that real (inflation-adjusted) average weekly earnings were up 2.2% in January over the prior year. That is more than double the 1% increase we saw in January of 2025.

Real income growth is the life blood of our economy, because that is what fuels real consumer spending growth, which accounts for two thirds of our overall economic growth. This is the most encouraging development I have seen year-to-date on the economic front. It is the most positive rate of change, aside from the broadening and strength in corporate earnings growth.

data.title: Exelon: Not Just A Bet On AI Data Center Tailwinds, But On Electrification Overall
data.description:
Introduction

Exelon Corporation (EXC) just reported its Q4 and full-year results that pleased investors, as the company’s outlook is fueled by data center tailwinds and a massive increase in capex for the next few years to support AI data center infrastructure.

By The Numbers

Sales came in at $5.41B, down around 1% y/y, and missed consensus by $80m. Three out of the four revenue segments showed quite good performance, growing between 14.5% to 23.8%, while ComEd saw its revenues decline by around 40%, which explains the lackluster overall top-line performance. The management explained this 40% drop anomaly and said that it was largely a “pass-through accounting shift.” The reason for this is that wholesale energy prices dropped significantly compared to where they were just a year ago, so the segment’s revenues plummeted; however, since the company is not allowed to make a profit on the energy itself, the segment actually improved operationally because its operating income increased.

Speaking of operating profit, ComEd saw its adjusted operating income increase to $252m from $243m, so as we can see, the 40% decline in revenue is not affecting the company’s profitability. PECO’s adjusted operating earnings decreased to $162m from $196m, BGE’s to $181m from $175m, and PHI’s to $171m from $132m. PECO’s lackluster performance was attributed to higher taxes and contracting costs, while the other ones saw their profits increasing due to data center demand and “multi-year plan” rate reconciliations. Overall, the company’s adjusted EPS for the quarter came in at $0.59, beating estimates by 6 cents, while for the full year, EPS was $2.77 vs. $2.50 last year.

The company’s financial position at the end of the year was quite good, with over $626m in cash, against $47B in long-term debt, which doesn’t look very good, but it is quite normal to have such high amounts of debt for companies like EXC. Their business is very capital-intensive, so it is expected that the debt amount is very high. It is more of a strategic tool than anything else. The company’s utilities are essentially legal monopolies, which means they can set rates that will be able to cover the interest they pay on their debt, and since everyone needs electricity, the cash flow is highly predictable. Banks are much more willing to lend to EXC than to a startup. Speaking of cash flows, the CFO came in at $6.25B, while FCF came in at -$2.275B due to high capital expenditures for the year, which totaled $8.5B and a notable increase from last year’s $7B. So, I am not worried about the debt pile; it’s just another day at the office for the company.

On top of these results, the management gave us guidance for the full year ahead. Adjusted EPS will be in the range of $2.81-$2.91, which is around 6% growth y/y, while the consensus expected it to be $2.83 a share.

It seems that investors like the report, as the company’s share price increased on the day and has continued to rise ever since. The company’s share price is up around 10% since the report. The investors looked past the revenue miss and rewarded the company’s decoupling efforts from volatile energy prices. The company is becoming a lot more like an infrastructure play, which is much more predictable and less risky than if the company remained a power generator.

Comments On The Outlook

The insatiable hunger for power from hyperscalers is benefiting many companies, and there is no doubt that the companies that allow hyperscalers to achieve their AI dreams, like the energy companies and anything adjacent to energy, like infrastructure, will continue to reap profits going forward. The only growth industry at the moment is the data center and AI business. So, it is not a surprise that Exelon is looking to take advantage of this massive demand for data centers. The company announced $41B in capex spending through 2029 to support demand for power from new AI data centers, upgrade the grid so that it could withstand volatile weather patterns better, and integrate renewable energy resources, like wind and solar, into its existing distribution network.

I cannot blame the company for chasing data center money. It’s a no-brainer. A lot of the planned capex will go towards upgrading the grid to handle the massive, concentrated AI data center requirements. This is not a surprise. Hyperscalers massively increased their capex for 2026, so it is only natural to see that companies that are there to support the AI dreams are also looking to spend billions more as well. The pipeline includes 19 GW for data center projects, which is a ridiculous amount, to put it lightly. This amount of energy can power over 16m homes if we take this estimate at face value, which says that 1GW could power 876K households for one year. And all of that is going to some data centers instead.

So, the shiny new object of data centers is a massive growth factor for many companies, but besides that, EXC has other growth opportunities. Opportunities that may not be as lucrative as data centers but are still good enough to provide growth. Electric vehicle adoption will help increase a home’s electricity consumption by quite a bit. Some reports suggest up to a 50% increase in electricity consumption. Exelon is looking to spend heavily on managed charging infrastructure so that as adoption increases, people will be able to charge their cars safely without blowing any of the transformers.

Another positive growth comes from the increased demand for heat pumps. There is a big push in some states in the U.S. to switch from gas furnaces to heat pumps. So, this push can potentially shift massive amounts of energy demand onto the electric grid, especially during the winter peaks. So, upgrading the grid is a very good idea for the company, which will pay dividends in the long run.

Should You Invest In EXC?

I think the company is a good choice, not only because of the AI data center tailwinds for the next couple of years, but also because I believe the company’s future is certain. The global digitization and electrification efforts to get away from fossil fuels and into alternatives are going to continue to increase, and as the infrastructure builder of the future, the company is exposed to many different alternative power investments around the world.

I think you could view EXC as the pick and shovel seller because it is taking advantage of the AI gold rush by accommodating hyperscaler demand, and also you can look at the company as a pure play on electrification, since the world will continue to evolve, and that means a lot of power is going to be needed, and that power will have to go through EXC’s wires, no matter how the energy is being made, whether that is alternative fuels or fossil fuels.

So, if you would like more AI exposure or a pure electrification play, EXC could be a good choice as a long-term investment.

data.title: RQI: My Favorite And 8.4% Yielding Way To Invest In REITs Now
data.description:
Back in April, 2025, I wrote a bullish piece on Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI), where I had identified several catalysts that could propel both the monthly dividend and the share price higher from then-current level.

I have to say that so far my thesis has been playing out very well:

RQI has delivered strong total returns, outperformed the REIT index (VNQ), and announced a juicy dividend bump of ~12%.

Because of this dividend (or distribution) increase, RQI's forward yield now stands at an attractive level of 8.4%, which, without the registered increase, would have been much lower due to the notable share price growth.

In fact, we can even observe that RQI's yield has become more attractive than before (e.g., it has increased by ~14% over the past 12 months):

In the meantime, REIT space or VNQ has experienced the opposite effect with its compressing yields, which is completely logical given how the interest rate environment has evolved.

In this context, some might argue that there is probably a justified reason behind RQI's yield divergence from the rest of the REIT world and that, arguably, these distributions are unsustainable or, to some extent, funded by a return-of-capital component, which is not that uncommon in the high-yield CEF space.

Well, I have reassessed the investment case and arrived at the conclusion that RQI remains a very strong buy.

Let me explain.

Thesis Review

One of the key drivers behind my interest in leveraged CEF instruments that are biased towards REITs is attributable to the suboptimal REIT growth (return) prospects.

Namely, the prevailing macro dynamics do not bode well for strong REIT returns that can be enabled by falling long-term yields. Instead, such aspects as stubbornly high inflation, a weakening dollar, and deglobalization clearly support elevated long-term yield levels. We can easily observe this by looking at the yield curve's reaction function to the previous base rate cuts. While the front end of the curve has moved down, the 7+ year segment has remained largely unchanged.

All of this has also been reflected in relatively stagnant REIT/VNQ returns. So, the risk is that by opening pure-play REIT positions, we are likely to experience no meaningful price returns and, what is important to understand, no tangible yield either - e.g., currently, VNQ yields ~3.7%, which on an inflation-adjusted basis is clearly unattractive.

Now, this is where RQI comes into play.

The overall idea of RQI is to take long positions in high-quality REITs and add an external leverage overlay to enhance the underlying core yield. This solves the unattractive yield issue and introduces a more enticing upside potential in case of a sector-wide recovery (valuation multiple expansion).

Granted, the presence of leverage introduces the opposite risk as well, but given how cheap the overall REIT space is and the fact that the capital has started to shift from growth into value, I do not see downside risk as a high-probability event. Plus, let's also remember that RQI portfolio primarily consists of well-capitalized, large-cap investment grade REITs, which help de-risk the situation:

On top of that, about 18% of RQI's holdings are tied to fixed income and/or preferred securities that are issued by the same (mostly) investment-grade REITs.

Speaking about the recently increased dividend and its sustainability, there are two items that are worth appreciating.

First, as of now, RQI has ~30% of its asset base fueled by external leverage, which has become an even bigger advantage as the probabilities of incremental base rate cuts this year have gone up. The lower the base rates, the cheaper it gets for RQI to service the debt, which, in turn, provides a direct positive support to the dividend coverage.

Second, the dividend coverage itself has been very robust, meaning that RQI has not tapped into its NAV base in order to keep the distributions running. Instead, there is a balance between net interest income generation and realization of long-term capital gains that supports RQI's distributions.

In fact, in December 2025, RQI was even able to distribute an extraordinary (supplemental) dividend of $0.13 per unit on top of the regular one without utilizing any ROC proceeds. And this happened right before the ~12% bump was made for the regular monthly dividend, which is a clear testament to RQI's ability to accommodate the current yield in a sustainable fashion.

The Bottom Line

The recent distribution hike has been fully justified, going well in line with the underlying cash flows and financial dynamics. The dividend coverage statistics (NII and long-term capital gain heavy) confirm that this is indeed the case, where the most important thing to appreciate is the fact that no ROC proceeds have been involved in the distribution process.

On a go forward basis, RQI should be able to sustain the current ~8.4% yield in exactly the same, sustainable manner, where such aspects as investment grade bias and likely lower base rates should come in handy.

The only thing that can materially derail RQI from the current track is a sudden plunge across the entire REIT market, which in that case would neutralize the long-term gain component that is currently playing a meaningful role in the overall dividend equation.

However, I view such a risk as a low probability event given how relatively depressed the REIT prices are and considering that the lion's share of the RQI portfolio is tied to either IG corporates or fixed income/preferred share securities. On top of that, let's also remember that a falling base rate environment should provide a tailwind for RQI on its NII per unit generation front, increasing the role of the NII component for facilitating the dividend payments.

In a nutshell, I remain bullish on RQI.

data.title: Crocs: Cleaning Up Wholesale, Share Repurchases At A Deeply Discounted Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

Crocs (CROX) closed out its fourth quarter with flattish revenues, with both the top and bottom lines above expectations thanks to better inventory management, direct-to-consumer (DTC) growth, and margin stabilization. The core Crocs brand showed stability, especially outside the U.S., while the HEYDUDE brand continued its turnaround with wholesale cleanup and selective promotional strategies. With strong free cash flow and aggressive share repurchases, at a valuation that’s below 7x forward earnings, I think Crocs is a buy.

A Look at the Most Recent Quarter

Crocs closed out 2025 with strong results that beat on both the top and bottom lines. In Q4, revenues clocked in at $958 million, down 4%, but that was an improvement sequentially from Q3. The Crocs brand generated $768 million, driven by 11% international growth. While this was partially offset by an 18% decline to $189 million at HEYDUDE, margins held up relatively well, which allowed for a 38-cent beat on EPS to $2.29 in the quarter.

When looking at the full-year results, FY’25 revenues were just over $4 billion, down 1.5% compared to last year as the company navigated some volatility across its brands. At Crocs, which accounts for 82% of total company revenue, full-year revenues were up only 1%, with the DTC business climbing 3% and wholesale channels dipping 1%. The reason for this was deliberate efforts to manage promotions and inventory flow.

In terms of the economics, units sold for the Crocs brand climbed 2% to 129 million pairs, with average selling prices holding roughly flat. Regionally, North America saw a 7% drop to $1.7 billion, driven by reduced DTC promotions and controlled wholesale sell-in. International revenues climbed 11% to $1.6 billion thanks to 23% DTC growth and 5% wholesale growth. In the international business, the big standout was China's sales growth, up 30% compared to last year.

For the HEYDUDE brand (the boat-shoe-style line they bought a few years ago), which accounts for the remaining 18% of total company sales, revenues were down 14% compared to last year as DTC edged up 3% because of new store openings and digital strength. Offsetting this was wholesale, which fell 27% due to channel cleanup and moderated performance marketing. HEYDUDE's average selling prices rose 4% to just under $32, but units declined 17% to 22 million pairs.

While negative growth across both wholesale channels might be disappointing at first glance, I actually believe these are the right steps for the company over the longer term. In its largest market in North America, the Crocs brand has deliberately slowed down how much product they ship to wholesale partners by avoiding overstocking, keeping prices stable, and protecting the brand from being discounted and being perceived as cheap. This essentially meant fewer wholesale sales compared to last year as the company focused more on selling to customers online or in their own stores (which grew this year).

Margins Are Stabilizing

At HEYDUDE, the actions were similar in that the company took back excess inventory from retailers and gave allowances for returns or markdowns. They also reduced heavy advertising spending that wasn't working well. Rather than pushing volume at any cost, the strategy around the wholesale business is to fix messy inventory piles in stores. That’ll most likely improve how the brand looks on shelves and set up a healthier, more profitable base for future sales.

In the meantime, though, margins have taken a small hit. For example, total company gross margins for the full year were 50 bps lower than last year at 58.3%, and while that included a 130 bps impact from tariffs, lower sourcing costs did help to offset this. The Crocs brand's gross margin was 61.3%, slipping 30 bps, while HEYDUDE's stood at 44.8%, off 290 bps (again, more work had to be done in wholesale). The margin compression was also felt further down the income statement as SG&A was 7% higher compared to last year, and operating margins of 22.3% were 330 bps lower than last year.

In terms of key highlights from the quarter and year, Crocs had a strong holiday season and had good customer reception to new clog innovations like the Unfurgettable Clog and extensions in the Echo and Baya franchises, which reinforced the brand’s leadership in clogs (still 74% of the mix). On the earnings call, management noted that Sandals approached $450 million in sales, representing 13% of the portfolio and capturing notable market share gains in North America with an extended selling season. Jibbitz and personalization accessories held steady at 8% of revenues, driven by elevated charms and new bag collections. With an ongoing NFL deal, a sold-out Stranger Things collaboration, a high-resale Classic Twilight clog, and the newly announced global LEGO starting early this year, there’s a number of partnerships that should help drive sales and amplify engagement.

With a still-strong free cash flow profile and a deeply discounted share price, management has been using its cash flow to buy back stock. With FCF generation at $660 million, $128 million was spent on debt reduction, and $577 million was spent on buybacks. With buybacks equaling roughly 10% of shares outstanding, the company has quickly become a true “share cannibal.” This hasn’t come at the expense of the balance sheet, however, as the company had $130 million in cash with over $900 million in revolver capacity. With another $747 million remaining on the repurchase authorization and leverage at 1.2x, I’d expect continued buybacks at current prices.

An Attractive Setup for 2026

As for the outlook, management took the Q4 call to provide guidance for 2026. For this year, the company expects revenues to range from down 1% to up slightly, with the Crocs brand expected to hold flat to up 2%, with 10% international growth that offsets anticipated North America softness as the company laps those 2H'25 actions like promo pullbacks and inventory management.

For HEYDUDE, the projection of a 7% to 9% decline feels pragmatic, but I believe it underpins a turnaround narrative with a return to growth in the second half, as those cleanup efforts fade and new products like Stretch Jersey gain traction.

Overall, this guidance suggests a year of stabilization rather than aggressive expansion, but with adjusted operating margins modestly expanding from 22.3% and EPS in the $12.88 to $13.35 range, it tells me the focus on cost efficiencies and buybacks would yield upside if demand is steady against tariffs.

Short-term, the first quarter looks like a continuation of recent headwinds, with revenues guided down 3.5-5.5%, Crocs dipping low single digits led by international, and HEYDUDE facing a steeper 15% to 18% drop. That to me implies a back-loaded year, with second-half revenue growth outpacing the first half, potentially turning positive as North America Crocs sees slight run-rate improvements and HEYDUDE laps easier comps. By prioritizing channel health over forced sales, the business will be in a better spot for much easier comps come 2027.

Longer-term, I think there’s good growth drivers with international expansion, where markets like China, Japan, and India offer multi-year runways with current shares at about a third of established levels. In my view, with a $100 million cost savings initiative, split between COGS and SG&A, I think Crocs’ management has enough firepower for investments in AI and other cost efficiencies. With capex modest at $70-80 million, higher than the $51.2 million in FY'25, this is still a figure less than 2% of total company revenues.

Risks and Valuation

In my view, the primary risk to the investment thesis for Crocs centers on the persistent softness in North America for the core Crocs brand, which accounted for a 7% revenue decline in 2025 and is expected to face ongoing headwinds this year. How this geography performs is pretty critical to the underlying business results, and if demand doesn't stabilize in a spending environment where lower-end consumers stay cautious, it could drag overall growth and delay a return to positive growth.

While there are tariff concerns (estimated at an $80 million impact this year) and concerns that competition could always put pressure on margins, I think the valuation compensates you for much of the risk here. At 6.5x EV/EBITDA and 7.9x P/E, the company trades at half the multiple of peers at 11.1x EV/EBITDA and 17.1x P/E (source: Bloomberg). Even against its own valuation, Crocs looks inexpensive, trading right in line with its own historical EV/EBITDA valuation range.

Share Repurchases Are a Catalyst

What I like about the Crocs thesis is that you don’t really need the Crocs brand to grow in order for the stock to work. As long as the company continues trading around this level below 10x earnings, management will likely continue buying back 10% of shares year in and year out. And if we do indeed have a business here that’s stabilizing, EPS can likely continue growing at a high-single-digit CAGR just because of share repurchases alone. Any growth that might come out of a turn in North American sales or continued international growth would just be the cherry on top.

With sell-side estimates where they are, you can see how quickly EPS could grow and how pathetic the valuation looks at these levels. At 6.9x forward earnings today, Crocs is likely trading at a sub-6x earnings multiple just two years out as a result of buybacks.

Bottom Line

Crocs is a story of patience that I think will pay off. The company’s careful approach to managing inventory, controlling wholesale, and focusing on direct-to-consumer sales may have slowed revenue growth in the short term, but I think it strengthens the foundation for long-term profitability. Coupled with robust free cash flow and aggressive share repurchases, there’s a natural catalyst for earnings growth and realizing value even if the top-line growth is modest.

Going forward, I expect that upside to come from international markets, product innovations, and ongoing cost efficiencies. But even without a big surge in sales, the combination of a disciplined strategy and an undervalued stock makes Crocs a name I’m comfortable owning. And I think the market may be underestimating just how powerful that setup might be.
